title: Shrirampur : टाकळीभानमध्ये कट्टा दाखवून मॅनेजरला कोंडले
description: श्रीरामपूर |प्रतिनिधी| Shrirampur तालुक्यातील टाकळीभान येथील देशी दारू दुकानात मॅनेजरला गावठी कट्ट्याचा धाक दाखवून खोलीत कोंडत रोख रक्कम व सोन्याची चैन असा एकूण 1 लाख 88 हजार रुपयांचा मुद्देमाल लुटून नेल्याची घटना घडली. पोलिसांनी दिलेल्या माहितीनुसार, नरेश रामास्वामी दुलम (वय 44) हे टाकळीभान येथील आर. एन. कापसे यांच्या मालकीच्या देशी दारू दुकानात गेल्या 20 वर्षांपासून […]
content:
श्रीरामपूर |प्रतिनिधी| Shrirampur



तालुक्यातील टाकळीभान येथील देशी दारू दुकानात मॅनेजरला गावठी कट्ट्याचा धाक दाखवून खोलीत कोंडत रोख रक्कम व सोन्याची चैन असा एकूण 1 लाख 88 हजार रुपयांचा मुद्देमाल लुटून नेल्याची घटना घडली. पोलिसांनी दिलेल्या माहितीनुसार, नरेश रामास्वामी दुलम (वय 44) हे टाकळीभान येथील आर. एन. कापसे यांच्या मालकीच्या देशी दारू दुकानात गेल्या 20 वर्षांपासून मॅनेजर म्हणून कार्यरत आहेत. घटनेच्या दिवशी त्यांनी आपल्या गळ्यातील सुमारे 10 ग्रॅम वजनाची सोन्याची चैन दुकानातील ड्रॉवरमध्ये ठेवली होती. त्याच ड्रॉवरमध्ये दुकानातील 48 हजार रुपयांची रोख रक्कमही ठेवलेली होती.



दुपारी साडेचारच्या सुमारास आराम करत असताना इकबाल उर्फ सोन्या शेख हा त्यांच्या खोलीत आला. त्याने एक लाख रुपयांची खंडणी मागितली. दुलम यांनी पैसे देण्यास नकार दिल्यानंतर आरोपीने कंबरेला लावलेला गावठी कट्टा दाखवत जीवे मारण्याची धमकी दिली. तसेच स्वतःवर अनेक गुन्हे असल्याचे सांगून दहशत निर्माण केली.



यानंतर आरोपीने दुलम यांना गोदामाच्या खोलीत कोंडून ठेवले. त्यानंतर दुकानातील काउंटरवर जाऊन तेथे बसलेल्या दिलीप येवले यांनाही धाक दाखवत गल्ल्यातील 48 हजार रुपयांची रोकड आणि ड्रॉवरमधील सोन्याची चैन बळजबरीने घेऊन तो पळून गेला. घटनेनंतर दुलम यांनी आरडाओरडा केल्यावर एका ग्राहकाने त्यांची सुटका केली. याप्रकरणी नरेश दुलम यांच्या फिर्यादीवरून इकबाल शेख उर्फ सोन्या (रा. टाकळीभान) याच्याविरुद्ध भारतीय न्याय संहिता कलम 2023 प्रमाणे 309 (1),308,327, 351 (2), 351 (3),3,25 प्रमाणे गुन्हा दाखल करण्यात आला आहे. या धाडसी लुटीमुळे टाकळीभान परिसरात भीतीचे वातावरण निर्माण झाले असून आरोपीचा शोध घेण्यासाठी पोलिसांनी तपासाची चक्रे वेगाने फिरविली आहेत.

title: Notion killing Skiff-influenced email app since most users use AI agents instead
description: Notion is "going all in on using agents to run your inbox."
content:
In February 2024, Notion bought Skiff, an encrypted email and productivity software startup. Within a year, Notion shut down Skiff’s email service (taking @skiff.com email addresses with it). And in April 2025, the San Francisco-based company released Notion Mail, a Gmail client primarily built by people who joined Notion through the Skiff acquisition. Today, Notion announced that it’s shutting down Notion Mail, effectively killing what little remained of Skiff email.
In an X post (first spotted by 9to5Mac) today, Notion said that it will shutter the Notion Mail “inbox across web, desktop, and iOS on September 22.”
The post claimed that most Notion users don’t use email clients anyway and instead rely on AI agents to handle their electronic correspondence. It reads:Read full article

title: Google finally releases a Finance Android app, promises iOS version later in 2026
description: It took 20 years, but the Finance app arrives just in time to be packed full of AI.
content:
Google Finance is not a new product—it has been around for 20 years, long enough that it initially relied on Flash to display charts and graphs. The website has gotten a few major updates over the years, but it has never had a mobile app until now. Google has released the first standalone app for Google Finance, which is currently exclusive to Android, with iOS planned for later this year.
The app is available globally in the Play Store, but that's not the only update to Google's financial tracker. The AI-powered makeover for the Finance website is also leaving beta, making Google's chatbot a core part of the experience. Naturally, the mobile app includes a heaping helping of generative AI that aims to make sense of irrational financial markets.
If you've checked out the new Finance web experience, you'll see a lot of familiar features in the app. You can create watchlists, monitor real-time market data, and keep up with financial news in one place. While perusing graphs of stock performance, Finance will use AI to generate "key moments" that can explain why the numbers changed. This feature initially launched in the Finance web interface in May.Read full article

title: Tailored for all? Uneasy truths about marketing gender-fluid fashion
description: As a marketing scholar who's passionate about fashion, Maren Hoff noticed so many professional women wearing suit vests to work that she purchased a few herself.It may seem that gender norms are loosening, as women adopt male-sounding names and men wear nail polish. Indeed, fashion and workplaces seem to reflect a shift: During the past decade, retailers and brands such as Nordstrom, Marc Jacobs, and Gucci have launched gender-fluid collections, and some companies have relaxed dress codes to allow more gender-fluid expression at work.But the way these trends play out often reinforce, rather than upend, traditional power hierarchies, says Hoff, an assistant professor at Harvard Business School.That’s because uptake heads primarily in one direction: Women are more likely to adopt masculine styles than vice versa, likely because masculinity signals power and feels less risky than feminine markers. It may be easier as a woman to wear a vest than for a male colleague to come to work wearing dangly earrings, for instance.Hoff’s research, forthcoming in the Journal of Consumer Research, finds:During the past century, about 50% more traditionally male names in the US became gender-fluid in an average year than female names.Traditionally masculine style has influenced women’s apparel more than the inverse, according to an analysis of clothing sold by a large online retailer.“Despite this trend being there on the marketplace to serve inclusivity, it actually does perpetuate those power dynamics,” says Hoff, who wrote “The Asymmetry of Gender-Fluid Trends” with Silvia Bellezza, associate professor at Columbia Business School. “There's this power hierarchy that has traditionally existed, and in one way or another, still appears to exist.”Consumers blur traditional gender lines“Symbolic consumption”—choosing products and brands to express one’s identity or signal status—has long interested Hoff. “You can study these trends in names and learn something about how society thinks about certain tastes at any given point in time, and then that becomes relevant to marketing and fashion,” she says.Gender fluidity is a relatively recent phenomenon, says Hoff, but one gaining traction: A 2023 report found that 36% of US consumers have purchased clothing outside traditional gender categories. Hoff points to her survey of over 1,200 consumers in which respondents said a truly gender-fluid collection should look balanced—representing about a 50-50 split between traditionally male and traditionally female styles.The researchers began by analyzing how designers, brands, and popular culture define gender fluidity. By mining Google search results, they found that gender fluidity takes shape gradually over time and differs from gender bending (adopting names or products designed for the opposite sex), unisex (designed for both genders), and androgyny (combining elements of both genders in one name or product).“Many people associate gender-fluid fashion with a particular community [such as LGBTQIA+], but that’s not the case,” the paper explains. “Gender-fluid fashion is for everyone.”Also Read: Consumers are warming up to fluid fashion as gender stereotypes take backseatWhen names cross from boys to girlsThe researchers hypothesized that masculinity continues to “socially dominate” femininity both culturally and in commerce, despite efforts to reduce gender inequity. They conducted six studies: two large dataset analyses and several follow-up experiments.Girls' names are more likely to defy genderResearchers asked participants to rank how likely they would be to choose particular names for a baby girl or boy, scoring their answers from zero (least likely) to 15 (most likely).The authors analyzed the US Social Security records of the top 100,000 baby names between 1880 and 2022. They looked for names clearly associated with either men or women when they first appeared and charted whether and how the names transitioned to being used by the opposite sex over time.In 1883, five boys and no girls were named Blake. In the 1960s, the name started to become popular among girls, and in 1981, the team determined it had achieved crossover status. The researchers found:More male names crossed gender lines. On average, 310 male names per year became gender fluid.Fewer female names crossed over. By comparison, only 207 female names per year made the shift.Menswear influences women’s fashionThen, the researchers took their thesis to fashion, looking at how often an image-recognition model miscategorized menswear as womenswear and vice versa. Since the model is trained to recognize traditional gender norms, gender-fluid designs might confuse it, the researchers suspected.They scraped over 200,000 product images from global online retailer Farfetch and found that:7.1% of womenswear was mistaken as menswear5.3% of menswear was mistaken as womenswearWomenswear is more likely to be misclassifiedWhile a model accurately categorized most clothing images by gender, womenswear was more often misclassified (7.1%) than menswear (5.3%).Additional studies probed underlying motives. Ultimately, they found that women and non-binary people were less scared of negative reactions and more aware of male advantage than men. For that reason, they’re more likely to choose a gender-fluid product.In settings that challenge traditional hierarchies—like workplaces that prize feminine traits—men feel more comfortable adopting gender-fluid options.How to build awareness among business leadersWhy do women appear more open to gender fluidity? Hoff says it’s less risky—and potentially conveys an advantage—for women to send in a resume with a fluid name and to wear fluid outfits than it is for men.“It speaks to this idea that women are trying to assimilate more with the traditionally powerful gender, and those traits are being rewarded,” she says.Based on the team’s findings, Hoff says that business leaders and marketers could:Reexamine the traits their workplace rewardsIf companies want employees to feel comfortable expressing themselves—including through gender-fluid clothing—the research suggests their leaders must look at how they define success. In one of Hoff’s studies, participants were more likely to dress fluidly at workplaces where firms emphasized more traditionally “feminine” traits like collaboration and empathy over assertiveness and competition. “That would be a workplace context where men would also be more open to dressing more fluidly,” Hoff says.Reduce perceived risk around inclusive productsFor fashion brands, consumer adoption depends in part on whether gender-fluid styles feel socially acceptable. Hoff says brands could consider advertising that encourages consumers to “be bold and take risks,” and suggests featuring more men in gender-fluid clothing to help destigmatize those choices.Recognize that it takes more awareness to dismantle hierarchyThe research shows that merely selling inclusive products can still reinforce traditional power dynamics if uptake flows primarily toward masculine-coded signals.Understand the gender associations of all productsProducts from drinks to sports cars carry strong gender connotations that companies can challenge or reinforce, deliberately or unintentionally. If women are always bridging that divide, entrenched power systems are unlikely to change.“Gender associations are so important,” Hoff says. “The implications are broader than the context that we study.”

title: Anthropic says Alibaba must be punished for largest Claude cloning attack
description: Alibaba allegedly used 25,000 accounts to mine Claude over 28.8 million exchanges.
content:
Anthropic has accused the Chinese firm Alibaba of launching the largest attack yet attempting to clone Claude, as China races to match the capabilities of Anthropic's leading model following Mythos' release and subsequent restriction from foreign markets.
Ars obtained a June 10 letter sent to Senators Tim Scott (R-SC) and Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) one day ahead of a Senate committee hearing on “AI and the American Dream.” In the letter, Anthropic shared “new, confidential evidence of the largest campaign to illicitly extract Claude’s capabilities we have ever measured.”
The attacks occurred between April 22 and June 5, when “operators afﬁliated with Alibaba and Alibaba Qwen, Alibaba’s AI lab” allegedly generated “more than 28.8 million exchanges with Claude through almost 25,000 fraudulent accounts,” Anthropic said. Violating Claude's terms of service and access restrictions, this campaign “targeted some of Claude’s most valuable capabilities, such as agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon tasks.”Read full article
Comments

title: Planet orbits so close to its star that their magnetic fields connect
description: At the right point of the orbit and stellar cycle, the star's chromosphere brightens.
content:
For most of human history, our view of "close to the Sun" was defined by the orbit of Mercury, with its 88-day orbit and barren, baking surface. But from the moment we started discovering exoplanets, it became very clear that our own Solar System was anything but a guide to the rest of the galaxy. Planets with orbits only a few days long are strikingly common, with the proximity to the star creating things that seem bizarre from our perspective: metal vapor in the atmosphere, or atmospheres puffed out to ridiculously low densities.
Now, we can apparently add an additional oddity: overlapping magnetic fields. Researchers have found a star/planet combo that experiences periodic brightening, which they ascribe to the interactions between the magnetic fields of both bodies.
Looking for repetition
This is one of those cases where theory came before discovery. People had already proposed that a planet orbiting close to its host star could interact with it if its magnetic field were sufficiently strong. And, in a number of cases, researchers have found evidence that this is happening, with one case of an extremely young star emitting flares seemingly in response to the orbit of its innermost planet.Read full article

title: Why Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia are more than just movie stars
description: Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia both admit they didn’t have much of a choice while starting out in movies. It was a matter of grabbing every opportunity they got, especially since they did not belong to film families.“Yes, I gained weight. But if someone would have asked me to chop off a finger, I would have done that too,” says Pednekkar, who put on 24 kg for her debut film, Dum Laga Ke Haisha (2015). Bhatia, on the other hand, made an unusual move by venturing down South early in her career, starring in Telugu and Tamil films although she was not familiar with the two languages. “For someone who is not from the film industry or does not have any connections, the only way is to see what you are getting and make that the best opportunity,” says Bhatia, who starred in the pan-India box office sensation Baahubali (2015).The two actors shared the stage at the Forbes India W-Power Self-Made Women event held in Mumbai on May 20, and spoke about creating a distinct identity for themselves in the entertainment world, navigating the challenges that come with stardom and their entrepreneurial experiences.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026“When I started my career, for me, success was also survival. It still is. I am lucky that I get to do something that I love,” says Pednekkar, who completed a decade in the industry last year. “Following a formula or a path that has been walked on is easy. Doing something that hasn’t been done scares people. And especially if you are a woman who’s walking your own path, you will scare society. They will constantly try to push you into a corner, they will try to box you.”Concurs Bhatia, who has had a two-decade journey in films already. “Success was not something that came immediately. But I knew I am going to enjoy doing this every day. And I just kept doing it, and there was one day when it worked,” she says.“I guess there are different pressures on actors who are from the film industry. But the difference is that I did not get a chance to prep. My prep was on location, on a film set. I had to learn two languages, Tamil and Telugu. I took the long road. I never took short cuts. But that helped me sustain.”Success also brought with it intense scrutiny and pressure. The two actors have been body-shamed at various times in their careers. They concede it is tough to deal with such trolling but have now learnt to stay immune to it.“It’s not about being actors… women are constantly picked on, commented on, because you are never meeting a beauty standard that has been set by men. Now, I really don’t care. I cannot let objectification or negativity affect me,” says Pednekkar, emphasising that public figures, in particular, are dehumanised. “The fact remains that anybody’s opinion about anybody is basically a reflection of them, and not a reflection of who I am,” points out Bhatia, who has chartbusters such as ‘Aaj ki Raat’ and ‘Ghafoor’ to her name.Though not a trained dancer, Bhatia fell in love with it over the years. She sees dance performances on screen as a celebration of women in their skin. “In fact, people forget movies, but they don’t forget songs. They are powerful and I will continue doing them,” she says.If dance is Bhatia’s passion, Pednekkar has been a climate crusader from a young age. She was named one of World Economic Forum’s Young Global Leaders a couple of years ago and has also invested in startups providing sustainable solutions.“I grew up with climate anxiety. I did not know I had it because mental health wasn’t ever discussed or spoken about back then,” she reveals. “I believed, as a child, that the world is going to reach a catastrophic point and it has. What felt like a fictional situation then is a reality we are living today. And it’s only going to keep getting worse. We have to ensure that we fight for our basic rights—clean air, clean nutrition, clean water.”Also Read: Tamannaah Bhatia's affair to rememberIn 2025, Pednekkar launched Backbay, a self-funded, premium beverage brand. She concedes she has probably entered the toughest category within the FMCG world. “It is one of the riskiest things I have done, but also one of the most gratifying ones,” she says, adding that every day as a founder is a new challenge.Bhatia, who turned entrepreneur with Tamannaah Fine Jewellery in January, believes once you become a businessperson you have to face every single fear of your life all the time. “I wanted to build an Indian brand that has a strong design language, and gives you style and volume. The retail business is what I want to work on and hopefully have it all over the country and globally too,” she says. “It’s important to ensure that your business makes money.”The two stars believe in the power of performance and hope to continue breaking boundaries along the way.

title: The antiseptic liquid mistake that is hurting you more than you realise
At over 50%, Russia’s share in India’s oil imports hits a record high in June
description: Russia accounts for more than half of India’s crude oil imports in June, surging to its highest share as cheap discounted barrels have displaced traditional Gulf suppliers against the backdrop of the war in West Asia.Russia’s share of India’s crude imports climbed to 52.5 percent in the first 22 days of June, up from 41.7 percent in May, according to an analysis of data from LSEG, a financial data provider.How India’s crude oil suppliers changedRussia's June figures are also a significant leap from the 22.4 percent share seen at the start of the year. The gains have come at the expense of India’s traditional Gulf suppliers. Saudi Arabia, for instance, has seen its share collapse to just 4.2 percent in June from nearly 21 percent in February, just before the war in West Asia broke out. Kuwait, Qatar and Iraq, which together accounted for over 30 percent of India’s imports in January, have effectively dropped to zero in June, shows LSEG data.Another notable addition in India’s crude oil suppliers this year is Venezuela. After US-imposed sanctions since 2019 kept Venezuelan barrels off India’s books, the South American producer has re-emerged as a supplier, capturing a 4.6 percent share in June. Though Venezuela’s June share is lower than those recorded in April and May, when they exceeded 5 percent, it has emerged as the third largest crude oil supplier to India. Before the US sanctions were imposed, Venezuela accounted for roughly 5-7 percent of India’s total crude oil imports.Also Read: How have commodity prices moved as Iran and the US begin talks in Europe?Effect of the West Asia warThe conflict in West Asia has been a key driver of the shift in India’s crude oil import profile. Its outbreak on February 28 injected uncertainty into transit via the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world’s seaborne oil passes. Before the beginning of the war, India used to get roughly 2.1 million barrels of crude per day through the Strait, making it the fourth-largest destination after China, South Korea and Japan.As the Iran-US standoff over the Strait of Hormuz intensified, it nudged Indian refiners to diversify away from Gulf-dependent supply chains.But that could change if the US-Iran peace agreement falls into place, paving the way for Iranian crude to re-enter global markets and easing concerns over the safe passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz. An estimated 93 million barrels of non-Iranian crude remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf, according to shipping analytics firm Kpler. Saudi Arabia accounts for the largest share at 42.5 million barrels, followed by UAE-origin crude at 18.4 million, Kuwait and Qatar at 17.1 million, and Iraq at 15 million barrels. A further 72 million barrels of Iranian crude, held west of Chabahar, could add to a global supply once the Strait is fully reopened.India’s total crude imports through June 22 stood at 16.3 million metric tonnes. Crude imports for May were 2 percent higher than the same period last year. This was a significant rebound after imports declined 13 percent in March year-on-year when the war broke out in West Asia.

title: Why Indian consumers can’t yet connect to satellite internet
description: In March 2025, Airtel signed an agreement with Elon Musk’s SpaceX to explore bringing Starlink, its satellite internet service, to India. Reliance Jio announced a similar agreement with SpaceX a day later.Eutelsat OneWeb, in which the Bharti Group is a major investor, operates another satellite constellation and has already secured key approvals to provide satellite communication services in the country. Jio, too, has partnered with Luxembourg-based SES through JioSpaceFiber and is evaluating the development of a sovereign low-earth orbit satellite constellation.Yet, Indian consumers still cannot buy satellite broadband services at scale as spectrum rules, security clearances, pricing mechanisms, and regulatory frameworks are still being put in place by the government.On June 17, the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) released draft rules governing the administrative assignment of spectrum under the Telecommunications Act, 2023. However, the proposed framework stopped short of laying out a complete operating framework for satellite broadband providers.On June 24, the government released the Authorisation Rules, 2026, replacing India's licensing regime with a new authorisation-based system for telecom services.For satellite operators, the developments over the last two weeks are relevant because they help define the framework under which telecom and satellite services will operate. "If I speak broadly about satellite communications, this is the first time India has taken a clear policy path," says Narendra Singh, founder and CEO of investment research firm Growth Investing.At the same time, the announcements do not immediately translate into commercial services for consumers. Questions around implementation, operating conditions and commercial rollout remain under discussion.For now, that means that while satellite internet remains a technology that is generating significant interest, it is yet to become widely available to consumers in India.What is satellite internet?In India, people access the internet through mobile towers or fibre-optic cables. When a user streams a video, sends a WhatsApp message or joins a video call, the data typically travels through terrestrial infrastructure such as mobile towers, fibre networks and data centres.For satellite internet, instead of relying entirely on infrastructure built on the ground, data is transmitted through satellites orbiting the Earth. Users connect through specialised terminals that communicate with satellites overhead, which then relay information to ground stations connected to the wider internet.While satellite internet has dominated telecom discussions over the past year, the technology itself and the demand for it are not new. "I wouldn't call it sudden, the need [for satellite internet] has always been there," says Anku Jain, managing director of MediaTek India. "What has genuinely changed in the last 18 months is the shift from geostationary (GEO) to low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites."Unlike traditional GEO satellites positioned much farther from Earth, LEO satellites orbit much closer to the planet, reducing latency and improving performance. The shift has made satellite internet a more realistic option for broadband connectivity, particularly in locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers is difficult or uneconomical.Why is there interest in satellite internet?India had more than 1.09 billion internet subscribers as of March 2026, according to data released by the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI). Of these, around 652 million were in urban areas, while about 441 million were in rural India.Yet, coverage challenges remain in remote locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers can be difficult. "The biggest challenge it addresses is coverage in locations where deploying terrestrial infrastructure is difficult due to geography, population density, or economic viability," Jain says.Industry executives do not view satellite internet as a replacement for fibre broadband or 5G networks. "Honestly, I do not see satellite as a competitor to telecom. The two are complementary," says Singh of Growth Investing. "Satellite will never replace fibre or 5G in urban India because terrestrial networks remain significantly cheaper and more efficient."Instead, satellite connectivity is expected to be used in locations where conventional infrastructure is difficult to deploy, as well as for applications such as maritime connectivity, disaster response and remote enterprise operations.Industry experts point to a combination of regulatory and operational hurdles that must be overcome before consumers can access satellite internet. "The bottleneck isn't any one thing. It is the sequential nature of all of them together," Jain says. Operators require spectrum, security clearances, gateway infrastructure, operating approvals and compliance with regulatory requirements before services can be launched commercially."The biggest bottleneck remains regulatory and operational readiness rather than technology," says Vinish Bawa, partner and telecom sector leader at PwC India. "Spectrum assignment frameworks, security compliance requirements, gateway approvals, coordination with multiple agencies, and clarity on commercial operating conditions all need to be finalised before services can scale."Another issue is the cost of the service. While operators are yet to announce final retail tariffs for India, satellite broadband may be more expensive than conventional broadband services. In late 2025, Starlink's India website briefly displayed a monthly subscription price of Rs8,600 and hardware costs of around Rs34,000 before the company withdrew the figures, saying they were test data and not final India pricing.When could consumers get satellite internet?While neither the government nor operators have announced a launch timeline, industry executives expect the first services to arrive before satellite broadband becomes mainstream.Bawa expects initial commercial launches and enterprise deployments over the next six to 12 months, subject to final regulatory clearances and spectrum assignment. However, he cautions that consumer adoption will take longer as operators build distribution networks, install gateways and reduce equipment costs.Meanwhile, Singh expects pilot deployments to begin in 2026 and enterprise adoption to scale in 2027. Meaningful consumer adoption, he says, is more likely to emerge around 2028, with mass-market adoption taking place later in the decade."The government wants assurance that national interests will be protected. The companies want policy certainty and speed," Singh says.

title: EngageX 2026: Spotlighting customer experience in the era of AI
description: As artificial intelligence reshapes customer interactions across industries, business leaders are racing to explore how organizations can harness technology without losing the trust that underpins customer relationships.This concern sat at the heart of Exotel EngageX 2026, presented by Forbes India and CNBC-TV18. Bringing together leaders from banking, insurance, aviation, logistics, retail, technology, and digital-native businesses, the event explored how customer experience is evolving from a support function into a strategic driver of growth, loyalty, and enterprise value.The evening opened with a discussion aptly titled Trust is Not a Brand Attribute, It's an Economic Asset. Moderated by CNBC-TV18's Latha Venkatesh, the conversation brought together leaders from the BFSI sector and Exotel, the leader in AI-led customer experience solutions and cloud communications for enterprises.During the discussion, Shivakumar Ganesan, Co-founder and CEO of Exotel, emphasized, "Technology certainly helps, but ultimately, it must help humans." Tarun Chugh, MD & CEO of Bajaj Life, described trust as a "ledger" built through every customer interaction, while Dhiraj Relli of HDFC Securities argued that reliability and availability remain the foundations of trust in a digital-first world. Alok Aggarwal of Muthoot Homefin distilled trust-building into a simple formula: commit, deliver, and repeat consistently.The focus then shifted to the future of intelligent customer experiences in Building CX That Learns, Predicts and Compounds. The panel explored how organizations are moving from reactive customer service to predictive engagement. Vijaybahu Joshi of Reliance Jio argued that enterprises often overlook regional and cultural nuances in customer behavior, while Marina Datta of Birla Opus stressed the need for hyper-personalization built on data. Swiggy's Rohan Chhazed highlighted how customers increasingly expect brands to anticipate their needs before they articulate them.One of the recurring themes throughout the day was that customer experience itself is becoming a key differentiator. That idea came through strongly during The Experience P&L Panel, where leaders from DTDC, Zepto and Exotel debated how CX should be measured. Retention emerged as the dominant metric. DTDC CEO Abhishek Chakraborty noted that every year begins with a clean slate regardless of past success, while Zepto's Divesh Sawhney described repeat transactions as the ultimate validation of customer loyalty. Exotel's Sachin Bhatia pointed to customer effort score as a leading indicator of future retention, reflecting growing consumer impatience and rising expectations.Perhaps the most talked-about moment of the evening came when Shivakumar Ganesan demonstrated what Exotel described as India's first autonomous AI call. Unlike conventional AI-assisted interactions that require human orchestration, the demonstration featured an AI agent independently initiating conversations, gathering information, and scheduling follow-ups. The live showcase offered a glimpse into a future where AI agents may increasingly act on behalf of both businesses and customers.The event also featured a series of fireside conversations that highlighted how customer experience transformation is unfolding across industries. In a conversation titled When Business Becomes a Best Friend, Rajesh Dogra, Chief Customer Experience Officer at Air India, discussed the airline's ongoing transformation journey following its return to the Tata Group. He revealed that Air India has worked to reimagine more than 37 customer touchpoints across the travel journey, from booking to baggage collection.The BFSI sector, where trust remains paramount, featured prominently throughout the evening. Girish Sehgal of ICICI Lombard outlined how AI is helping accelerate claims processing, strengthen fraud detection, and improve underwriting precision. Vijay Rajagopal of AWS argued that India's digital public infrastructure and vast transaction volumes provide an unparalleled testing ground for AI innovation at scale.Trust and transformation also formed the backbone of discussions with banking leaders. Rama Mohan Rao Amara, Managing Director – Retail Business & Operations at SBI, spoke about the institution's enduring commitment to customer centricity while calling for greater agility within regulated environments. Meanwhile, IDFC FIRST Bank's V. Vaidyanathan offered a compelling reflection on trust during times of crisis, recounting the bank's swift response to a high-profile fraud incident and emphasizing that public confidence is earned through decisive and transparent action.The evening concluded with the unveiling of the cover of a book titled Architects of CX, Exotel's annual initiative that brings together the voices behind the best in customer experience. The book showcases the stories behind the scenes: the challenges these leaders navigated, the opportunities they spotted, and how they led the transformations that make AHA CX possible.The most striking takeaway from EngageX 2026 was that while AI may redefine how organizations interact with customers, trust remains the foundation upon which every meaningful customer relationship is built. At the end of the day, the tools may change, but the principles endure.To know more about Exotel, log on to https://exotel.com/Click here to watch more content: https://ms.forbesindia.com/exotel-engagex/The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Pragya Misra is building India's AI bridge
description: When Pragya Misra joined OpenAI as its first hire in India, there was no local entity, no office and no roadmap. But that didn’t bother her. “That was precisely the opportunity,” she says. “We had a chance to help define how one of the world’s most important AI markets would engage with this technology.”It wasn’t the first time she’d walked into a room that didn’t yet exist. She was among WhatsApp’s earliest hires in India, building its communications and advocacy infrastructure. At Truecaller, she led public affairs as the company navigated the complicated terrain of identity, privacy and trust at scale. Each role required the same core skill: Translating something technically significant into something politically and publicly legible.At OpenAI, that skill is now operating at a different order of magnitude. ‘The early work was about listening,” she says. “I spent time with developers, researchers, startups, educators, civil society organisations, business leaders and policymakers to understand where AI could create the most value and what responsible adoption would look like in India.”W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026That groundwork appears to have paid off. India is now OpenAI’s second-largest market by user base, with adoption growing more than threefold year over year. But what Misra finds more telling is where the growth is coming from. “Students, creators, educators, professionals, founders, non-profits and public institutions are all finding practical ways to use AI in their daily work,” she says. “That’s the clearest signal of a technology reaching an inflection point. It stops being something people experiment with and becomes something they rely on.”While Misra’s career—from EY to co-founding an IT company, to WhatsApp, to Truecaller, to OpenAI—looks eclectic on paper, she disagrees it is so. “There has always been a consistent thread,” she says. “I’ve always been drawn to moments when technology changes how society functions at scale.”The qualities that have carried her across those transitions were visible early. “When Pragya joined the team, she distinguished herself through her drive, focus and commitment to excellence. She consistently took ownership, delivered strong results, and built trust through effective collaboration with colleagues and clients,” says Rajiv Memani, chairman and CEO of EY India and managing partner of the Africa-India region, who worked with her between 2013 and 2014. “These qualities have fuelled her growth and continue to define her leadership today.”What the breadth has given her, she argues, is pattern recognition. “The diversity of my career has made me a generalist. It helps me connect dots, pattern-match across industries, and bring different perspectives together.”The launch of ChatGPT Go at ₹399 a month—a significant pricing concession for India—is one visible result of her work. “I don’t see accessibility and sustainability as opposing goals,” Misra says. “The most transformative technologies become sustainable because they reach large numbers of people and create meaningful value in their lives.”Also Read: Dr Sivaranjani Santosh's prescription for changeFor India specifically, she argues, the equalising potential of AI goes beyond price. Its multimodal and multilingual capabilities, she says, can help bridge barriers of language, literacy and access, bringing intelligence closer to those who have historically been left out of the digital economy. “When more people have access to intelligence, knowledge and capability, more people can learn, innovate, create and participate in economicopportunity.”Away from work, Misra is a former national-level amateur golfer, a meditation trainer and the host of Pragyaan, a podcast about ideas, decisions and what gives life meaning.Golf taught her that the game is won internally while meditation ingrained in her that clarity comes from stillness.Both feel relevant to the role she now occupies—shaping AI policy for a billion-plus people at a moment when most of them are forming their first impressions of what AI will do to their lives. “The challenge isn’t simply the pace of technological progress,” she says. “It’s ensuring that understanding, access and opportunity keep pace with it. If AI only benefits a small group of people, we will have missed one of the greatest opportunities of our generation.”What gives her optimism is India’s track record. The country has absorbed mobile internet, digital payments and digital public infrastructure at a scale and speed that surprised the world each time. She believes AI will follow the same arc—but with India doing more than adopting, and help defining it.“As technology transforms how we work, live and relate to one another, what will matter most is not just intelligence, but wisdom,” she says. “Machines may increasingly tell us what we can do. But the deeper question of whether we should do it, and why, will always remain profoundly human.”

title: Feds deny Polestar authorization to sell cars in US from model year 2027
description: Unlike with Volvo, there will be no authorization for Polestar to sell its cars here.
content:
The electric car brand Polestar's days in the US are seriously numbered. Today, the company revealed that the US Commerce Department has declined to authorize imports of new Polestars from model year 2027 onward as part of a rule banning connected cars from automakers with Chinese links.
Polestar says it will continue to sell its existing stock of Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 SUVs and "will continue to support customers, including providing access to its service network." But we can forget about the Polestar 5 sedan, the Polestar 6 roadster, or any future models making it to these shores.
The automaker was spun out of Volvo Cars several years ago as a pure EV brand by its corporate parent, Zhejiang Geely Holding, a Chinese company that also owns OEMs like Lynk and Co and Zeekr. And just weeks ago, Commerce authorized Volvo to import MY27 vehicles. At the time, Polestar told Ars that it was continuing to work with US authorities to meet the regulations; that work was evidently in vain.Read full article

Apple ratchets up prices, blames the cost of memory
description: Some Macs are hundreds of dollars more expensive today than yesterday.
content:
Apple bumped its prices across much of its product lineup today, in some cases adding hundreds of dollars to the cost of a new Macintosh. An entry-level MacBook Neo that cost $599 is now $699. The formerly $1,299 iMac will now be a $1,499 iMac. An M5 MacBook Pro that was $1,699 is now $1,999. And at the very high end, an M3 Ultra Mac Studio—which features 96GB of memory—sees a $1,300 price increase to $5,299.
The iPad line is also getting more expensive, between $100 and $200, depending on the model. Smaller price increases have been applied to products like the Apple TV and HomePod. The price of iPhones remains unchanged, at least for now.
The culprit? The soaring price of memory, according to an interview that Apple CEO Tim Cook gave to The Wall Street Journal earlier this month. “Unfortunately, price increases are unavoidable,” Cook told the paper. “We’re doing our best to mitigate the huge increases that are being passed to us, and we’ve been trying to shield our customers from the increases, but the situation has become unsustainable.”Read full article

title: The "sad inevitability" of Europe's heat wave
description: Europeans are baking under their second heat wave of the summer.
content:
Europe is in the midst of its second big heat wave of the year, and it’s breaking more records. France just recorded its hottest day ever, with temperatures exceeding 44° Celsius in some places. Around 40 people have drowned in local water bodies, likely attempting to escape the heat, and thousands more are without electricity.
As temperatures hit a sweltering 36° in some regions of the United Kingdom, schools canceled classes and train delays abounded. United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres described London as “cooking.” As the city hosts its annual Climate Action Week, the UK meteorological service has issued a red alert for multiple regions, signaling that exceptionally hot and humid weather is forecasted and likely to impact the general public. Switzerland and Spain have also issued warnings to residents.
Emma Howard Boyd, the former chair of the London Climate Resilience Review who now chairs the National Heat Risk Commission in the UK, said that when it comes to heat resilience in the country, the problem is not just homes—which are usually not air-conditioned.Read full article

title: New effort will get genome sequences for entire Endangered Species list
description: Colossal Biosciences will be biobanking tissues from all of them as well.
content:
The US Endangered Species Act compels the government to identify species at risk of extinction and devise plans to restore populations and the habitats they depend on. It has seen some spectacular successes, such as the restoration of the bald eagle to much of its original range. But over 2,300 plant and animal populations remain on the list, requiring ongoing government intervention.
On Thursday, it was announced that all of those species would see their genomes sequenced and tissue samples preserved to aid future conservation efforts. The work will be done by a partnership between two unexpected parties. One is the US government, which has generally attempted to undercut the Endangered Species Act as part of its anti-regulatory efforts. It is joined by Colossal Biosciences, a biotech company that has a controversial take on what actually constitutes a species.
Colossal has always said it had a conservation focus, but its headline-grabbing efforts have been directed toward restoring species that have been driven to extinction. It intends to do that by developing a combination of gene editing and reproductive technologies that it expects it can profitably license. But its dire wolf announcement, in which only a tiny handful of genetic changes were edited in to grey wolves, have raised some questions about its seriousness regarding these efforts.Read full article

title: Every Homo naledi we know of is female, and the implications are fascinating
description: "There is no natural explanation," says paleoanthropologist John Hawks.
content:
All the Homo naledi skeletons in Rising Star Cave are female, and that probably didn’t happen by accident.
In 2013, a team of anthropologists led by Lee Berger unearthed the remains of more than 20 small-bodied hominins (ancient relatives of humans), all 335,000 to 236,000 years old, from the Rising Star Cave System in South Africa. Excavations at Rising Star have sparked debate about whether these little hominins had all ended up in the caves by tragic accident, or whether they’d been carefully placed there by other members of their enigmatic species, dubbed Homo naledi. 
Now there's a plot twist that may speak to how the remains got there: All of the hominins in Rising Star are female, at least according to the proteins in their dental enamel.Read full article

title: ‘Found coping mechanism in ChatGPT…’: When healing becomes an act for the feed
Dr Sivaranjani Santosh's prescription for change
description: For years, Dr Sivaranjani Santosh’s work was not about court petitions, regulatory orders or social media campaigns. It was about helping parents avoid needing a doctor in the first place.A paediatrician based in Hyderabad, Sivaranjani built her practice around prevention. She taught parents first-aid and CPR, counselled mothers on breastfeeding and infant care, and spent years trying to equip families with the knowledge to make informed decisions about their children’s health. “My aim was always to empower parents with the right knowledge and parenting skills,” she says. “So they are capable of taking care of their children in the best possible way and reach out to us only when need be.”That philosophy would eventually lead her into an eight-year battle that brought her national attention and contributed to a significant change in the way sugary drinks are labelled in India.Medicine was never a backup plan. Although her family discouraged her from pursuing it because they believed the profession was too demanding, Sivaranjani says she never seriously considered another career. “From when I was a child, it was fixed in my head that I’ll become a doctor.”After studying at JIPMER in Puducherry and specialising in paediatrics, she worked in different clinical settings before settling in Hyderabad. At Birthplace Hospital, she introduced prenatal classes for expectant mothers and practices aimed at encouraging breastfeeding from the earliest possible moments after birth. Later, she began conducting first-aid and CPR training sessions for parents.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026The issue that would define the next chapter of her career emerged unexpectedly when a mother asked Sivaranjani whether a popular packaged drink was the same as oral rehydration solution (ORS). She answered instinctively that it was. Later, when she started seeing children with prolonged diarrhoea and worsening dehydration, she began asking parents to bring the drinks they were giving their children.What she found was very concerning. As she looked closely at the packaging of drinks being sold as ORS, she found that pharmacies were misleading customers by selling them drinks that did not conform to the World Health Organization’s recommended ORS formulation. What followed was years of letters to regulators, conversations with medical bodies, social media campaigns, and, eventually, a public interest litigation.Finally, in October 2025, the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) said that products not meeting WHO-recommended standards could not use the term ORS on their labels. “Then I cried and cried and cried,” she recalls the moment the order was passed. “It was such a relief for me.”The fight, however, was never simply about a product label. For Sivaranjani, it represented a larger question about public health, ethics and the responsibility of professionals to speak up against something wrong.Also Read: Sonali Ghosh: The keeper of KazirangaThe campaign also came at personal and professional costs. She faced criticism from sections of the medical community, resistance from industry stakeholders and legal notices challenging her public statements.Yet, colleagues say, persistence has long been one of her defining traits. Dr Chetan Ginigeri, director of paediatrics at Manipal Hospitals, Bengaluru, has known Sivaranjani for nearly three decades, dating back to their days as classmates in medical school. “She always stood for what was right,” he says. “She was never somebody who would shy away from expressing what she believed was right for the child and the family.”According to Ginigeri, people may have become aware of Sivaranjani because of the ORS campaign, but the work behind it was years in the making. He points to her efforts in community education, injury prevention and CPR training as examples of a commitment that extends beyond the consulting room.That commitment remains central to how Sivaranjani sees her role. Even after the regulatory orders, she continues to focus on educating parents and raising awareness about child health issues. In her view, the larger challenge remains ensuring that parents understand what they are buying and that products are marketed in ways that do not create confusion.She continues to engage with regulators, raise awareness on social media and track developments in the category. The work, she says, is less about a single product than about protecting the integrity of public health information. “I have to take this to its logical conclusion,” she says.Sivaranjani’s journey has been nothing short of extraordinary. The doctor who once taught mothers what to expect in the first days after childbirth now finds herself speaking to audiences far beyond hospital walls.

title: Graphic of the day: Stranded crude in the Gulf

title: Why do UK drivers pay more for fuel than in other European countries?
description:
Sharp rises in fuel costs have hit UK drivers' wallets, but that isn't the case everywhere

British drivers are paying significantly more at the pumps than drivers in several other European countries.
According to RAC data, petrol currently costs around 156p per litre in the UK, compared with roughly 136p in Cyprus, 130p in Spain and 116p in Malta.
Drivers don’t have to refuel too many times before the difference starts hitting their wallets. According to the RAC, filling a 55-litre tank in a typical family car now costs around £87 for petrol and just over £100 for diesel.
Elsewhere in Europe, Polish drivers pay just 122p per litre for petrol, while a litre of petrol costs Bulgarian motorists 131p. If European countries access the same global oil market, and face the same geopolitical challenges, why are petrol and diesel so much more expensive in the UK?
For Luke Bosdet of the AA, the answer is simple. “It's a tax thing,” he said. “Some countries load their tax onto car ownership – car tax and the like. Others load it on to car use – duty on fuel. And others combine the two.”
At this week’s average petrol price of 152.7p per litre, UK drivers pay 26p in VAT and 53p in fuel duty, according to the RAC. That means 79p of every litre sold is tax, equivalent to just over half of the pump price.
Compare that with Spain, where the cost of a litre of fuel is around 130p. RAC figures show why Spain is cheaper. The fuel and retailer margin account for about 76p in both countries. But rather than the 79p in duty and VAT paid by UK drivers, those in Spain pay 53p.
The Malta outlier
But there are outliers too. In Malta, for example, petrol costs just 116p per litre. Yet based on RAC figures, tax accounts for 56% of the pump price, not dissimilar to the UK tax take despite the much lower pump price.
However, if British drivers think they are hard done by, spare a thought for Dutch motorists who pay 193p per litre of petrol and 182p per litre of diesel.
For Stuart Adam, a senior economist at the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS), “the UK is roughly in the middle of the pack in terms of prices and slightly above average in terms of tax rate”.
He said that 20 years ago, the UK had “easily the highest tax rates in terms of both petrol and diesel” but “a series of real-term cuts to fuel duties” has meant that’s no longer the case.
So why is petrol and diesel cheaper in Spain, Malta, Poland and Bulgaria? Steve Gooding, director of the RAC Foundation, said: “More often than not, the fact that fuel prices are cheaper in southern and eastern European countries is likely to reflect government policies, including state-owned fuel production, designed to keep fuel prices down where average wages are also relatively low.”
Take Malta, for example. A spokesperson for the Central Bank of Malta told Autocar that fuel prices have remained stable in recent years due to a "fixed energy price policy". Through energy subsidies, retail fuel prices have been "maintained at fixed levels despite fluctuations in global oil prices", the spokesperson said.
Duty and VAT: why doesn’t the government cut it?
In the UK, however, the focus has been on fuel duty. Adam said ministers could reduce it if they wanted to, but it would come at a cost.
"The obvious downside is that it costs the government money and the government has been scratching around for revenue in difficult circumstances for some time," he said.
Fuel duty and VAT currently raise around £30 billion a year for the Treasury. Adam noted that if this revenue is reduced, the government would have to raise money elsewhere or accept a lower tax take.
Others think ministers should start by cutting VAT on fuel. The RAC Foundation has calculated that since the US-Iran war began in February, the government has taken more than £741 million extra in VAT from higher pump prices, as part of what it calls the “war premium” in terms of the higher costs motorists have faced.
But in Spain, the government reacted to the war in Iran by temporarily reducing VAT on fuel from 21% to 10% in March. Gordon Balmer, executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, said VAT is “a tax on a tax” because, unlike fuel duty, the amount of VAT collected rises whenever pump prices increase. He added that the government could do something similar to Spain if it wanted to.
“Instead of announcing summer giveaways such as cheap meal deals and days out at the zoo, the government could have helped everyone by introducing a 10% cut in VAT on fuel,” he said.
Adam disagrees. He argues that removing VAT from fuel duty would make very little difference if the overall tax take remained the same. He said: “As things stand we put VAT on the price of fuel including the duty. However, you could equally well have VAT on the price excluding the duty and make up the revenue with a higher duty rate. But you would end up in a very similar place.”
In response, the Treasury told Autocar that the 5p fuel duty cut has been extended until the end of the year and that mileage rates for people who drive for work have increased from 45p to 55p. It also pointed to Fuel Finder, which is designed to help drivers identify the cheapest forecourt nearby.
Taxes don't tell the whole story
Yet fuel prices are only one part of the cost of motoring. Professor Nigel Driffield, a professor of strategy and international business at Warwick Business School, said: “It is inadvisable to look at one tax in isolation.” He added: “You can compare excise duty and VAT in different countries, but if cars or companies are taxed differently, then the aggregate impact may differ.”
Balmer agrees. He said that several European countries rely much more heavily on toll roads than the UK. "You can actually say the price of fuel is lower, but don't forget they also charge road tolls. If you are looking at it in terms of motoring, you need to take all of the various taxes into account," he said.
For Adam, the bigger question is not how fuel taxes compare across Europe, but why governments tax fuel in the first place.
“The more fundamental question is why we have a specific tax on fuel or, for that matter, a specific tax on driving and cars at all?” he said.
Adam said fuel taxes should not simply be used “to raise revenue or support particular groups of motorists”. Instead, he argued that governments should use them to reflect the wider costs of driving, “including carbon emissions, air pollution and congestion”.
Whether motorists agree is another matter entirely.

title: Sonali Ghosh: The keeper of Kaziranga
description: At 4.30 am in the wild pockets of Assam, much before sunlight has hit the thick elephant grass, Sonali Ghosh is at work.The animal kingdom has begun to stir, and by 5.15 am, the first set of tourists will enter the Kaziranga National Park for a unique safari experience: Riding aboard trained elephants for a majestic vantage point, which lets you get up close with the famous one-horned rhinoceros.Assam is home to about 80 percent of the world’s population of the greater one-horned rhino and in the last year, a record 5 lakh tourists came to Kaziranga to see them. This included 30,000 international tourists, a 100 percent jump over the previous year.Ghosh, who made history in 2023 when she was appointed the national park’s first female field director, manages not only the hospitality and tourist experience, but also the annual floods, threats of poaching and the challenging ecological habitat, along with engaging the local community in conservation efforts. A large part of that comes from helping them gain sustainable livelihoods such that they are not dependent or encroaching upon forest resources.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026“When people think of forest service, they think of this romantic undertone of living amidst nature,” Ghosh, who has now served in the Indian Forest Service for 25 years, says. “But to maintain that forest is something else altogether. Each season brings its own challenges.”Ghosh remains busy until sunset, when the evening safari visitors have exited the park. But work doesn’t end there—after a short break, field patrolling begins.In tourist season, it’s about looking at hospitality, how to engage visitors, whether the safari experience and roads are up to par, and that nothing untoward is taking place. When the park closes for the monsoon, they have to gear up for floods. Then the entire focus becomes about whether the animals are moving safely to the highlands and then returning to the park later; how to reduce mortality; whether the staff is alright.Having served at two Unesco World Heritage Sites—Assam’s Kaziranga and Manas National Parks—Ghosh says her work uniquely blends culture, nature, communities and wildlife. “I’ve been fortunate to get strong on-the-job learning of how to connect people for conservation.”This particular strength led her to becoming the first Indian to win the prestigious WCPA (World Commission on Protected Areas)-Kenton Miller Award in October 2025, which she was presented at the IUCN (International Union for Conservation of Nature) World Conservation Congress in Abu Dhabi.“Sonali Ghosh has pioneered a transformative model of governance in protected area management, exemplified by her leadership at Manas and Kaziranga National Parks,” Madhu Rao, chair, IUCN World Commission on Protected Areas, tells Forbes India via email. “She has championed the welfare of forest frontline staff—particularly by empowering newly recruited women rangers—and has fostered a culture of inclusivity and gender equality in conservation.”Also Read: W-Power 2026: How Kavitha Subramanian Helped Build Upstox for the Next Billion InvestorsThe wild sideWith her father in the army, Ghosh grew up amid India’s greenest areas—in Pune’s lush cantonment area, in the foothills of the Himalayas in Rishikesh (where her family home was), a stint in Shillong, which introduced her to the bountiful Northeast of India.Ghosh made several attempts at clearing the medical entrance exam, but didn’t make it, which pushed her to a regular science degree. “I wanted to have a specialism, and I applied to the premier Wildlife Institute of India [Dehradun], which had a rigorous examination. I was fortunate to be one of the 10 students admitted, and that was the turning point,” she says.She appeared for the Indian Forest Service examination thereafter, and the rest is history. Ghosh was posted in Kaziranga in 2002-03 for training.“In fact, I was one of the first women probationers serving in Kaziranga. So, everybody was a bit confused about what to do with me,” she recounts.The path wasn’t without its hurdles, however. “One aspect was the cultural and social pressure of getting married and having children at a certain age, and whether and how to do that while living in a forest,” she says.The other challenges were more technical, pertaining to being among the only women working in the remote area. “Everyday things, like how do you stay overnight in a forest as a woman? There were no closed bathing areas. Do you just go out and have a bath?”Ghosh’s compassion for animals was inculcated early on through her parents, who would encourage her to rescue and adopt stray dogs and cats.Kaziranga also boasts the highest density of the Bengal tiger in the world, and more than 90 percent of the wild buffalo population. Rhino poaching remains an important issue in the region, even making and breaking state governments.“Around the recent Assam elections, we were proud to showcase that we have almost neutralised the factors around rhino deaths that are human-related,” she says. “There’s still much work to be done. But we’re happy to say that the rhino population has increased from 25 in 1905 to more than 3,000 today.”Wildlife conservation, Ghosh says, is a lot more than about animals. It’s also largely linked to local communities, including tourists.The government’s National Education Policy has been beneficial to the cause, since it mandates student internships. “Last year, we were happy to have more than 80 students from local colleges who interned with us. We’ve also been talking to the private sector about getting more volunteers from their workforce interested in wildlife and supporting us in various parts of management,” she adds.More than its magnificent Big Five—the rhino, tiger, elephant, buffalo and swamp deer—Ghosh wants for Kaziranga to be known as a champion of wildlife conservation. “Kaziranga should be known as the epitome of sacrifice of the forest frontline,” she says. “A ground which the soldier walks on, and one that has also been able to restore the endangered species and bring it back to glory.”
